android.widget.CheckBox.text: Prescription Delivery bringing PrEP straight to my door
android.widget.CheckBox.resource-id: Prescription Delivery bringing PrEP straight to my door_cjrn2MzMIlys6v6rErlS_4_5v1kf84zfgp
android.widget.CheckBox.heading: false

android.widget.TextView.text: PrEP (pre-exposure prophylaxis) is medicine that reduces your chances of getting HIV from sex or injection drug use. When taken as prescribed, PrEP is highly effective for preventing HIV.
android.widget.TextView.heading: false

android.view.View.text: Are There Different Types of PrEP?
android.view.View.heading: true

android.widget.TextView.text: Since its introduction to the world over a decade ago, PrEP has only become better with each passing year. So much so that different versions have been introduced, from pills to injectables, to accommodate anyone’s lifestyle and preference.
android.widget.TextView.heading: false

android.widget.TextView.text: If you are sexually active and HIV-negative, then yes! Being on PrEP will help give you peace of mind and will put you in control of your sexual health!
android.widget.TextView.heading: false

android.widget.TextView.text: PrEP is available as a pill and an injectable. When speaking to your healthcare provider, we recommend asking about the benefits of both to decide which is right for you!
android.widget.TextView.heading: false

android.widget.TextView.text: While everyone can experience medications differently, PrEP has a strong track record of people experiencing little to no side effects and is extremely safe. Your healthcare provider will monitor you every step of the way and always be around to answer any questions you have.
android.widget.TextView.heading: false

android.widget.TextView.text: We're not a medical clinic! Our focus is on HIV testing, education, and reducing barriers to accessing PrEP. This includes covering copays for provider visits or prescriptions, providing transportation to medical appointments, ensuring prescriptions can be delivered directly to you, making referrals, assisting with insurance enrollment, and helping you understand insurance benefits. Learn more here:
android.widget.TextView.heading: false

android.widget.TextView.text: CDC recommends that everyone between the ages of 13 and 64 get tested for HIV at least once in their lifetime. For people with certain risk factors, CDC recommends getting tested at least once a year. CDC recommends testing at least once a year for syphilis, chlamydia, and gonorrhea. Those who have multiple or anonymous partners should be tested more frequently (e.g., every 3 to 6 months).
android.widget.TextView.heading: false
